coredata.dc:title: Synergetic dual co-catalyst and interfacial bonds in N-CQDs/NCN/BNQDs to promote photoremoval of uranium(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qixu
Xiao, Qianxiang
He, Feng
He, Wen
Liu, Kai
Zhao, Chongkun
Chang, Ziyang
Wang, Hongqing
coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic efficiency of a catalyst is dependent on two main factors: the separation of excitons and the migration of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, we employed a strategy of defect engineering, co-catalyst co-modification and interfacial chemical bonding synergistic modification to prepare N-CQDs-x/NCN/BNQDs-y by introducing nitrogen defects in graphitic carbon nitride (NCN) and concomitantly incorporating boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) onto its surface. And the N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 showed excellent photocatalytic removal of U(VI), which achieved 98.7 % after 120 min of illumination. Furthermore, the catalytic activities were ranked as follows: N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 > N-CQDs-15/NCN > NCN/BNQDs-1 > NCN > g-C3N4. Systematic investigations unveiled that the improved catalytic performance of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 was mainly attributed to the nitrogen defects in g-C3N4 facilitating carrier separation, the C–C interface bonding between N-CQDs and g-C3N4 and the hydrogen bonding between BNQDs and g-C3N4, as well as co-catalysts transferring electrons and holes, respectively, promoting the simultaneous transfer of e- and h+. Simultaneously, the prepared photocatalytic materials exhibited outstanding cyclic stability, with the U(VI) removal rate by the catalyst remaining above 91 % after five consecutive photocatalytic experiments. This study not only presents an effective photocatalytic material for the remediation of uranium-containing wastewater but also offers insights into the design of efficient photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Synergetic dual co-catalyst and interfacial bonds in N-CQDs/NCN/BNQDs to promote photoremoval of uranium(VI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic efficiency of a catalyst is dependent on two main factors: the separation of excitons and the migration of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, we employed a strategy of defect engineering, co-catalyst co-modification and interfacial chemical bonding synergistic modification to prepare N-CQDs-x/NCN/BNQDs-y by introducing nitrogen defects in graphitic carbon nitride (NCN) and concomitantly incorporating boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) onto its surface. And the N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 showed excellent photocatalytic removal of U(VI), which achieved 98.7 % after 120 min of illumination. Furthermore, the catalytic activities were ranked as follows: N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 > N-CQDs-15/NCN > NCN/BNQDs-1 > NCN > g-C3N4. Systematic investigations unveiled that the improved catalytic performance of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 was mainly attributed to the nitrogen defects in g-C3N4 facilitating carrier separation, the C–C interface bonding between N-CQDs and g-C3N4 and the hydrogen bonding between BNQDs and g-C3N4, as well as co-catalysts transferring electrons and holes, respectively, promoting the simultaneous transfer of e- and h+. Simultaneously, the prepared photocatalytic materials exhibited outstanding cyclic stability, with the U(VI) removal rate by the catalyst remaining above 91 % after five consecutive photocatalytic experiments. This study not only presents an effective photocatalytic material for the remediation of uranium-containing wastewater but also offers insights into the design of efficient photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium is an important raw material for nuclear energy, which is radioactive and non-renewable, so how to effectively remove and recover uranium from uranium-containing wastewater not only reduces environmental pollution, but also contributes to the renewable use of resources[1]. Up to now, a variety of techniques have been developed for the removal and recovery of uranium, including ion exchange, adsorption, membrane separation and photocatalysis[2–4]. Photocatalytic reduction technology has gained increasing attention in recent years since it reduces water-soluble uranium(VI) (U(VI)) to non-soluble uranium(IV), eliminates secondary contamination, and does not require the consumption of other energy sources[5,6].

Among many photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride has attracted significant research interest in the field of photoremoval of U(VI)[7–9]. Due to its wide optical response range and appropriate band gap (∼2.7 eV), as well as various excellent properties, such as stable structure and easy preparation[10]. However, pristine g-C3N4 has limitations in terms of low efficiency of carrier separation and electron-hole migration efficiency, which significantly hinder its further application in photoremoval of U(VI). Previous reports have demonstrated that the loading of co-catalysts on the surface of photocatalysts is beneficial for the transfer of photogenerated carriers[11,12]. And the following two scenarios of co-catalytic modification of g-C3N4 have been used so far. One is to just facilitate the transfer of photogenerated e- in the g-C3N4 through the use of a co-catalyst with electron-harvesting properties (Fig. 1
a). For example, Dai et al.[13] designed carbon dots-modified g-C3N4 to effectively facilitate the photogenerated electron transfer, resulting in 1.1 times higher U(VI) removal than that of pristine g-C3N4. Another is to just promote the transfer of photogenerated holes in the g-C3N4 by using a co-catalyst with hole trapping properties (Fig. 1 b). Li et al.[14] constructed enrichment sites for photogenerated holes by modifying boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) on the Fe-doped g-C3N4, thereby achieving a photodegradation efficiency for tetracycline that was 21.6 folds higher than that of g-C3N4. It is well known that the carrier including both electron and hole components. Therefore, the promotion of carrier migration should not only consider the single migration of electrons or holes, but also the simultaneous migration of electrons and holes. However, the use of dual co-catalysts has not been reported to synergistically promote the transfer of electrons and holes in g-C3N4, thus enhancing its performance in photocatalytic removal of U(VI). More importantly, both of the aforementioned quantum dot-modified g-C3N4 photocatalytic removal of U(VI) are a quantum dot modification and the quantum dots are simply complexed with g-C3N4 [15]. Although it can further promote the separation of e- and h+, a high Schottky energy barrier exists between the quantum dots and g-C3N4 interfaces, which has a hindering effect on the transfer of carriers[16]. Excitingly, the formation of electronic bridges by creating interfacial bonds between the co-catalyst and the catalyst is an effective strategy to address this issue[17]. If a chemical bond is formed between the interfaces, i.e., a bridge for carrier transfer is formed at the interface, the Schottky barrier between the interfaces will be significantly lowered promoting efficient interfacial electron transfer[18].

Furthermore, the dissociation of excitons within the g-C3N4 constitutes a significant factor influencing its photocatalytic efficiency in the removal of U(VI). It has been demonstrated that the incorporation of defects into g-C3N4 can alter the electronic structure, as well as making the defects traps for photogenerated electrons, thus boosting carrier separation[19]. Concurrently, the defect regions is rich in delocalized electrons and coordination-unsaturated atoms, which can coordinate with other materials to form interface chemical bonds[20].

As a novel zero-dimensional nanocarbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have excellent light absorption and electron transfer properties, can be used as co-catalysts[21]. In addition, it has been shown that doping carbon quantum dots with more electronegative N atoms can further enhance their electron transfer capacity by changing their internal charge distribution[22]. On the other hand, boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) is a zero-dimensional materials with a graphene-like structure. Importantly, BNQDs have negatively charged oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces, thus making their highly attractive to photogenerated holes due to electrostatic interactions, which enables their to act as hole extractors to construct hole transport channels on photocatalysts and thus facilitate the transfer of photogenerated holes[23].

Inspired by above facts, herein, a new photocatalyst with C–C covalent bonds and hydrogen bond, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/nitrogen defective g-C3N4/boron nitride quantum dots (N-CQDs-x/NCN/BNQDs-y), has been designed and synthesized by N-CQDs and BNQDs co-modified nitrogen defective g-C3N4. Notably, the dual-channel structure for electron and hole transport and interfacial C–C covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds, which generated by the dual-quantum-dot modification, and nitrogen defects synergistically and efficiently facilitated the exciton segregation and photogenerated carrier migration, which ultimately enhanced its photoremoval performance for U(VI).
All detailed of chemicals, experiments, characterization and DFT calculation were given in the Supplementary Material.

The crystal structures of the catalysts (g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN, and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1) were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. As the Fig. 2
a shown, all of the catalysts exhibit two characteristic diffraction peaks. Specifically, the pure g-C3N4 has two diffraction peaks at 13° and 27.6° representing its (100) plane and (002) plane respectively, i.e., planar periodic units of tri-s-triazine units and interlayer π-π stacking of aromatic structures[24]. However, the (002) peaks of NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 exhibit a slight rightward shift compared to the pristine g-C3N4. This indicates that the interlayer stacking distance of the catalyst decreases with the introduction of defects and co-catalysts[25]. Importantly, the peak intensity of (100) in NCN was remained almost unchanged, implying that the structure of g-C3N4 remains unchanged following the introduction of nitrogen defects. In the meantime, the crystal structure of NCN was negligibly altered after coupling with the N-CQD and BNQDs, which proved that N-CQDs-15/NCN and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 inherited the crystallinity of NCN, suggesting that its crystal structure was preserved.

Furthermore, the chemical features of the samples were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. And their FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 b) exhibited characteristic absorption bands centered at 3100–3300 cm−1, 1205–1640 cm−1 and 810 cm−1, which were attributed to the N–H and O–H stretching, stretching vibrations of C-N heterocycles, and bending vibrations of triazine units, respectively. Significantly, compared to pristine g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 all showed a new peak at 2177 cm−1, arising from C≡N groups[26].

And the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 2c) revealed sharp N1s and C1s peaks for all samples, while smaller O1s peaks were observed owing to the surface adsorption of oxygen-containing species. Meanwhile, weak B1s signals are detected in the N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1, confirming the successful integration of BNQDs onto the surface of NCN. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 2 d, the high-resolution C1s spectra of the g-C3N4 sample exhibit three absorption peaks, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C-NHx and C-N functionalities, with binding energies of 284.8, 286.4 and 288.2 eV respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution N1s spectra of the g-C3N4 sample reveal three absorption peaks, corresponding to N-Hx, N-(C)3 and C=N-C, with binding energies of 401.3, 400.4 and 398.6 eV, as shown in Fig. 2 e. As illustrated in Table S1 , the absorption peak area ratio of N-(C)3 (N3C) and C=N-C (N2C) decreased from 0.15 in g-C3N4 to 0.13 in NCN, indicating the presence of nitrogen vacancies in N3C [27]. In the meantime, the binding energy of N-(C)3 slightly shift to lower energies, possibly due to the introduction of nitrogen defects form cyano, whose N1s binding energy is between N2C and N3C [28]. As well, the EPR test results show that NCN has a significant absorption peak at g = 2.004 and its peak intensity exceeds that of g-C3N4, indicating defect formation and an increase in unpaired electrons (Fig. 2 g). In conclusion, it is shown that nitrogen defects and cyano-functional groups have been successfully introduced into the carbon nitride skeleton. Additionally, only two prominent absorption peaks were observed for N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 in the B1s spectra, which were attributed to the presence of B-O and B-N bonds with binding energies of 192.6 eV and 191.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 f)[29]. This result suggests that the BNQD surface contains oxygen-containing functional groups, which is in agreement with literature reports[30]. In other words, BNQDs were successfully integrated into the surface of N-CQDs-15/NCN.

Simultaneously, the specific surface areas of g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 were calculated from N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm to be 84.71, 67.70, 72.87 and 81.49 m2/g, respectively ( Fig. S1 ). Furthermore, all materials exhibit type IV isotherms and display H3 hysteresis loops (Fig. 2 h). It was evident that the specific surface area of g-C3N4 decreases after the introduction of nitrogen defects, likely due to the destruction of the mesoporous structure of pristine g-C3N4 [31]. Conversely, the specific surface area increase after loading N-CQD and BNQD, due to the excellent specific surface area of the quantum dots[32]. In addition, the decrease in pore volume of other samples compared to the original g-C3N4 may also be due to the loss of the g-C3N4 mesoporous structure as a result of defects (Fig. 2 i).

Then, the morphological structure of the catalyst was observed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From the Fig. 3 and Fig. S2 , the image show that pristine g-C3N4 exhibits a bulk structure, while nitrogen-deficient carbon nitride transforms into irregularly sheet structure. Moreover, the random distribution of N-CQDs and BNQDs on the carbon nitride surface can be observed through the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. As shown in images, the lattice stripe with a d spacing of 0.20 nm is consistent with the (101) crystal plane of graphite, whereas the lattice stripe with a d spacing of 0.21 nm coincides with the (100) crystal plane of boron nitride[33,34]. The elemental mapping images provided further evidence that the elements C, N, O and B were present in the N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 and were distributed uniformly on the surface of the photocatalysts. ( Fig. S3 ).

Subsequently, the optical absorption performance of the photocatalysts were evaluated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). The g-C3N4 exhibits its maximum absorption at 440 nm, whereas the absorption edge of NCN shifts to 460 nm after the introduction of nitrogen defects, as shown in Fig. 4
a. Moreover, the incorporation of N-CQDs onto the NCN surface significantly enhances its absorption range from UV to NIR, indicating a broadened spectral response attributed to the presence of N-CQDs. Compared with the N-CQDs-15/NCN, the absorption edge of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 shows a minor bule-shift, but they still demonstrate significant visible absorption. Besides, in Fig. 4 b, the band gaps (Eg) of g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 were determined via the Kubelka-Munk formula based on the Tauc plot, yielding values of 2.55, 2.45, 2.37, and 2.40 eV, respectively[35]. And the above results show that the defect engineering and loaded co-catalysts enhance the visible light absorption performance of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1. Furthermore, the Mott-Schottky diagram indicates that all the samples exhibit positive slopes, thereby confirming their classification as n-type semiconductors. Simultaneously, the flat-band potentials (Efb) measured at −0.92, −0.62, −0.76, and −0.68 V for g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN, and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 (vs. Ag/AgCl at pH = 7.04), respectively (Fig. 4 d-g).[36]. Meanwhile, the band gaps between the valence band (VB) and the Fermi energy levels (Ef) of the catalysts were determined to be 2.20, 1.96, 2.12, and 2.10 V, respectively, by the XPS valence band spectra (Fig. 4 c). Typically, the Fermi energy level (Ef) approximates the flat-band potential (Efb)[37]. Thus, the Fermi energy levels (Ef) of the photocatalysts were calculated to be −0.31, −0.01, −0.15, and −0.07 V according to the formula ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.198 eV + 0.059 pH, with valence bands (VB) at 1.89, 1.95, 1.99, and 2.03 V (vs. RHE), respectively[38]. Therefore, the conduction bands (CB) of the samples can be calculated to be −0.66, −0.50, −0.38, and −0.37 V (vs. RHE) from the formula of ECB = EVB − Eg. From the above results, it can be seen that the introduction of nitrogen defects and N-CQDs results in a narrower bandgap for the samples, accompanied by positive shifts in both the CB and VB. In contrast, the incorporation of BNQDs has a minimal effect on the bandgap. Additionally, the charge separation efficiency of the samples was further elucidated through photocurrent spectroscopy and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As expected, the photocurrent intensity and impedance arc radius follow the following sequence N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 > QDs/NCN > NCN > g-C3N4 and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 < QDs/NCN < NCN < g-C3N4, respectively, underscoring defect and dual quantum dot co-modification provide transport channels for electrons and holes, respectively, and improves photogenerated carrier separation and transport efficiency (Fig. 4h-i). That is, nitrogen defects effectively modulate the energy band structure of g-C3N4 and enhance photogenerated carrier separation, N-CQD and BNQD act as acceptors of e- and h+, respectively, and promote the transfer of photogenerated e- and h+, respectively. What’s more, the C–C covalent bond formed between NCN and N-CQD as well as the hydrogen bond formed between NCN and BNQD also facilitate the transfer of photogenerated electrons, as evidenced by the DFT calculations.

In order to elucidate the transfer pathways of electrons and holes subsequent to the coupling of NCN to N-CQDs and BNQDs, respectively, the work functions and charge density difference at the contact interfaces were calculated by density functional theory (DFT), and the optimised geometric model is presented in Fig. 5
a and Fig. 5 c. In the charge density difference of NCN/N-CQDs, the green and indigo part signals the charge depletion and accumulation, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 5 b. It was evident that the transfer of e- from the N-CQDs to the NCN, which is in accordance with the planar differential charge density ( Fig. S4 ). Meanwhile, the Bader charge calculations further indicate that the N-CQDs model transfers 0.126e to the NCN model. Similarly, the charge density difference illustrates that the transfer of e- from NCNs to BNQDs, and the Bader charge results show that the BNQDs model transfers 0.072e from the NCN model (Fig. 5 d). Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S5 , the calculated work functions of NCN, N-CQDs and BNQDs are 5.50 eV, 5.23 eV and 5.55 eV, respectively. In general, electrons tend to concentrate on the side with the larger work function in composites, thereby forming a built-in electric field between the interfaces[39]. These calculations demonstrate that when N-CQDs are recombined with NCN, NCN exhibits a more negative charge, whereas N-CQDs display a more positive charge. Consequently, photogenerated electrons are transfer from NCN to N-CQDs. In contrast, when BNQDs are recombined with NCN, BNQDs have more negative charges, while NCN has more positive charges. Therefore, photogenerated holes are transfer from NCN to BNQDs. Notably, in the charge density difference of NCN/N-CQDs (Fig. 5 b), the electron cloud density around the carbon atoms in N-CQDs has a distinct local distribution compared to that of the carbon atoms in NCN, indicating a strong bonding effect between C and C[40]. And the carbon atom in N-CQDs shows more charge depletion, while the corresponding carbon atom in NCN shows charge accumulation, indicating that the existence of C–C covalent bond between NCN and N-CQDs[41,42]. Likewise, after the BNQDs combine with NCN, the nitrogen atoms in NCN exhibit significant charge depletion, while the oxygen and hydrogen atoms on the BNQDs exhibit charge accumulation, suggesting the existence of hydrogen bonding (N•••H-O) between the BNQDs and NCN. Consequently, following recombination, the C–C covalent bond that would be formed between NCN and N-CQDs and acts as an electronic bridge, facilitating the transfer of photogenerated electrons from NCN to N-CQD, while the hydrogen bond formed between NCN and BNQDs promoting the transfer of photogenerated holes. Of course, this will ultimately improve the performance of photoremoval of U(VI) by N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1.

In order to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the samples, the photoremoval efficiency of U(VI) was examined under simulated sunlight irradiation (λ > 400 nm). As demonstrated in Fig. S5 , when the amount of NCN is fixed, the photoremoval efficiency of N-CQDs-x/NCN exhibits an initial increase followed by a subsequent decrease with the increase in the content of N-CQDs. The maximum removal efficiency is attained when N-CQDs was added at an amount of 150 μL, i.e., N-CQDs-15/NCN. A similar trend was observed in the removal efficiency of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-y, which increased and then decreased with the increase in the content of BNQDs. The highest removal efficiency was achieved when BNQDs was added at an amount of 1 m L, i.e., N-CQDs-15/NCN/ BNQDs-1.) Consequently, subsequent experimental investigations were conducted with N-CQDs-15/NCN and N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1.

Upon reaching the adsorption–desorption equilibrium (dark adsorption for 60 min), the adsorption rates of g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN, NCN/BNQDs-1, N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1, and N-CQDs-15/g-C3N4/BNQDs-1 were determined to be 3.4, 9.0, 9.4, 10.9, 16.9, and 6.4 %, respectively (Fig. 6
a). Without a photocatalyst, the concentration of U(VI) remains nearly constant even after 120 min of light exposure, which indicates that the autocatalytic reaction of U(VI) is negligible. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic removal of U(VI) by pristine g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN, NCN/BNQDs-1 and N-CQDs-15/g-C3N4/BNQDs-1were 22.2 %, 59.7 %, 79.7 %, 75.9 % and 68.5 %, respectively. Notably, the U(VI) removal rate reaches 98.4 % by N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 under the same conditions. In addition, the kinetic rate constant (k) for the photoremoval of U(VI) was calculated via a pseudo-primary kinetic model, as illustrated in Fig. 6 b. N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 composites (0.0316 min−1) exhibits the highest reaction rate, which was 15.3, 4.15, 2.35, 2.68 and 3.29 times that of g-C3N4 (0.00207 min−1), NCN (0.00762 min−1), N-CQDs-15/NCN (0.0135 min−1), NCN/BNQDs-1 (0.0118 min−1) and N-CQDs-15/g-C3N4/BNQDs-1 (0.00969 min−1), respectively. Furthermore, N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 not only shortened the photocatalytic removal time but also improved the removal efficiency of U(VI) compared with most of the previously reported g-C3N4-based photocatalysts ( Table S2 ). Overall, these results are in complete agreement with the photocurrent and impedance results described above, i.e. defect engineering, C–C covalent bonds, hydrogen bonding between BNQDs and NCN and dual channel transfer of electrons and holes effectively contribute to the photocatalytic properties of the material.

Additionally, previous reports have indicated that U(VI) exists in different forms at varying pH[43]. As a consequence, further testing was conducted to assess the photocatalytic removal efficiency of U(VI) at varying pH values, with the results presented in Fig. 6 c. At a pH of 5, the material demonstrated the optimal efficiency for the removal of U(VI), reaching a peak of 98.4 % after 120 min of illumination. However, both U(VI) removal efficiencies decreased as the initial pH of the solution was lowered from 5 to 3 and increased to 7, respectively. In order to further analyze the reasons for the variation of U(VI) photoremoval efficiency under different pH conditions, the zeta potential values of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 were measured. According to Fig. 6 d, the photocatalysts exhibited positive zeta potential values at a solution pH of 3, whereas those on the sample surface progressively became negative with increasing pH to 3.8 and became more negative as the pH increased further. Meanwhile, our previous studies revealed that U(VI) in solution at pH values below 5 is predominantly present in the form of UO2 2+ [44]. Therefore, at pH values below 3.8, the influence of electrostatic repulsion hinders the contact between the photocatalyst and U(VI), resulting in a gradual decline in the photoremoval efficiency of U(VI) as the pH value decreases[45]. In contrast, the electrostatic interaction between the U(VI) and photocatalyst is favourable for catalytic reduction when the pH value lies between 3.8 and 5. However, at pH levels exceeding 5, the existence form of U(VI) transitions from UO2 2+ to (UO2)3(OH)5 +, (UO2)4(OH)7 - and UO2(OH)3 -. Despite the persistently negative zeta potential values on the catalyst's surface at this time, the adsorption rate diminishes as the positive charge density of the (U(VI) decreases and progressively becomes more negative. Besides, the formation of U(VI) complexes reinforces ion stability, thereby enhancing U(VI) stability at pH levels above 5[46]. Consequently, the photoremoval efficiency of U(VI) is compromised at pH higher than 5. Furthermore, the valence states of uranium following adsorption and photocatalytic reactions were analyzed using XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 6e, the XPS spectra of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 following darkening exhibited two distinct absorption peaks, corresponding to U 4f5/2 and U 4f7/2 of U(VI), with binding energies of 392.6 eV and 381.8 eV, respectively, which were attributed to the adsorption of U(VI) on the surface of the photocatalyst. Following the photoreaction, two novel absorption peaks were observed in the XPS spectrum, exhibiting binding energies of 391.7 eV and 381.0 eV, respectively, which were attributed to U4f5/2 and U4f7/2 of U (IV)[47]. Meanwhile, the proportion of U(IV) and U(VI) in insoluble uranium is 48.5 % and 51.5 %, respectively. In addition, XRD results of the final product showed it to be studtite ((UO2)O2⋅2H2O) after photocatalytic treatment (PDF#35–0571) ( Fig. S7 )[8]. This also suggests that soluble U(VI) is transformed into insoluble U(IV) and then partially oxidised to insoluble U(VI).

There are currently two different views on the effect on oxygen in photoremoval studies of U(VI). One is that oxygen hinders the photoremoval of U(VI)[48]. The other is that a moderate amount of oxygen favours the photoremoval of U(VI)[49]. Consequently, to further explore into the influence of oxygen on the photoremoval of U(VI) in this system, we undertook photocatalytic experiments with N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 as catalysts under nitrogen, air, and oxygen, respectively (Fig. 6 f). The results illustrate a reduction in the photoremoval efficiency of U(VI) under both pure nitrogen and oxygen conditions compared to that under air conditions, signifying moderate amounts of oxygen facilitate photoremoval of dissolved U(VI). In general, this may be due to two reasons. One is the involvement of O2 in the transformantion of U(VI) to U(IV)[50]. And the other is that the conversion of U(IV) to (UO2)O2⋅2H2O also requires the participation of O2 [51], which is also consistent with the aforementioned XRD of the product. While excessive O2 may re-oxidize U(IV)[52].

In the meantime, in Fig. 6 g, in order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1, the photoremoval performance of U(VI) was tested by the addition of various metal ions (including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pd2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+) to the reaction system. The results demonstrate that it exhibited excellent U(VI) removal efficiency in systems coexisting with different metal ions, indicating its good anti-interference ability. Then, the stability of the synthesized photocatalysts was assessed through cycling experiments. At the end of each photocatalytic reaction, the reaction solution was filtered and the resulting catalyst was stirred overnight in NaHCO3 (0.1 M) solution, filtered and washed with deionised water, and then reactivated for reuse. After five cycles of desorption experiments, the catalyst maintained a U(VI) removal efficiency exceeding 91.3 %, with no significant decline in catalytic activity, thus confirming the excellent stability of the synthesized photocatalyst (Fig. 6 h). Concurrently, the crystal structures of the photocatalysts before and after the reaction were analyzed and compared using XRD, revealing no discernible difference were observed between the two and further illustrated the stability of the catalysts (Fig. 6 i).

To comprehensively dissect the relevant reactive species involved in the U(VI) photocatalytic reaction mechanism, DMPO acted as a spin trapping agent, and the presence of the relevant free radicals was detected using the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique. As depicted in Fig. 7
a-b, no discernible EPR signal was observed under dark conditions. Conversely, the distinctive EPR peak corresponding to •O2 – and •OH distinctly emerges under illumination, confirming the production of •O2 – and •OH by the catalyst. At the same time, capture experiments were conducted using methanol (MeOH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) as scavengers targeting h+, e-, •OH and •O2 –, respectively, with the results presented in Fig. 7 c. In the control experiment, the efficiency of photoremoval of U(VI) decrease significantly in absence of methanol, which could be caused by the reoxidation of U(IV) by •OH generated from the oxidation of H2O by h+ [53]. And in the presence of methanol, •OH could react with MeOH to form CO2 and H2O[54]. These results indicate that the methanol acts as both a scavenger of •OH and a hole depletory, thereby facilitating the separation of e- and h+ and ultimately improving the efficiency of U(VI) photoremoval. Additionally, after adding TBA to the reaction system, the photoremoval efficiency of U(VI) remained basically unchanged. However, a notable decline in the photoremoval efficiency was observed after the addition of AgNO3 or p-BQ, particularly the addition of AgNO3, which decreased to approximately 1.3 %. The above findings clarify that photogenerated electrons and •O2 – are key species in the photoremoval of U(VI) by N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1.

Consequently, building upon this foundation, we propose a reaction mechanism of the reaction process of photoremoval of U(VI), as illustrated in Fig. 7 d, with detailed elucidation provided by the following equation (eq (1)−eq (7). Under light excitation, the photocatalyst N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 generates electron-hole pairs. Subsequently, e- at the CB are transfer to the surface of N-CQDs via C–C covalent bonds, after which it reacts with U(VI) to form U(IV). Additionally, e- can interact with O2 in the reaction environment to produce •O2 –, which subsequently facilitates the conversion of U(VI) to U(VI). Furthermore, U(IV) is converted into (UO2)O2·2H2O by the combined action of e-, •O2 – and H+. On the other hand, h+ generated at the VB are transfer to BNQDs and oxidizes methanol to CO2 and H2O. Concurrently, part of h+ reacts with OH– to form •OH, which also oxidizes of methanol to CO2 and H2O.
(1) N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 + hυ → N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 (e- + h+)
(2) U(VI) + 2e- → U(IV)
(3) O2 + e- → •O2 –
(4) U(VI) + •O2 – → U(IV)
(5) U(IV) + •O2 – + H+ → (UO2)O2·2H2O
(6) OH– + h+ → •OH
(7) CH3OH + h+/•OH → CO2 + H2O

In this study, photocatalysts N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 with C–C covalent bonds were synthesized via defect engineering and co-modification with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) as co-catalysts. The findings indicate that the introduction of nitrogen defects creates energy levels within the carbon nitride, facilitating the dissociation of internal carriers. Moreover, the robust electron-absorption capabilities of N-CQDs and the exceptional hole-transfer properties of BNQDs help establishing dual channels for electron-hole transfer, thereby enhancing the transfer of e- and h+ and inhibiting their recombination. More importantly, the C–C bonds and hydrogen bonds formed between the defective g-C3N4 and the N-CQDs and BNQDs, respectively, eliminate the transport energy barriers between them and are more favourable for the transport of electrons and holes Consequently, the catalyst displays excellent photoremoval of U(VI) as a consequence of the aforementioned advantages. The N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 composites demonstrate a photoremoval rate that was 16.7, 4.5, 2.6, and 2.9 times greater than that of the pristine g-C3N4, NCN, N-CQDs-15/NCN, and NCN/BNQDs-1, respectively. Additionally, N-CQDs-15/NCN/BNQDs-1 also has good interference resistance and chemical stability. Overall, this study provides a new method for uranium extraction and removal and a new strategy for designing efficient photocatalytic uranium removal catalysts.
